skill.name: react-state-management
skill.description: Guide React developers in choosing and applying modern state management with Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, and React Query.
skill.summary: Choose and implement React state patterns for local, global, server, URL, and form state.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, browser storage examples, same-origin fetch examples, and documentation links. Review found no executable skill scripts, no malicious network endpoint, no credential exfiltration, and no prompt injection attempt; the remaining concern is that examples mention localStorage token handling, which implementers should review carefully.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fence delimiters and TypeScript examples, not executable Ruby or shell backticks. No evidence found of commands executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences and inert documentation snippets. There is no script file or runtime instruction that executes these examples.

low_findings.title: Same-Origin Fetch Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The fetch calls use relative API paths inside React state management examples. They do not contact an external domain or send secrets from the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both fetch calls are relative URLs in code samples. The skill contains no executable wrapper that would run them during skill use.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Are Benign Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are resource links to Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, and TanStack Query documentation. They are not outbound requests made by skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown links in a Resources section. They do not include tracking parameters, secret material, or code that opens them automatically.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Example Requires Implementer Care
low_findings.description: The documentation demonstrates theme persistence and token removal from localStorage. This is not malicious, but applications should avoid storing sensitive tokens in browser storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Browser storage access is clearly present in examples, but only as documentation. The token removal line implies a security-sensitive pattern that deserves a low-risk warning.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance alerts map to ordinary prose, table text, type fields, or React Query options. No evidence found of crypto algorithms or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no cryptographic function, hashing algorithm, host discovery command, or environment probing behavior.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined false-positive code execution with documentation-only fetch examples and browser storage examples. No evidence found of obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combined signals exist only in inert examples and Markdown formatting. Manual review found no executable path connecting command execution, network access, and credential access.

content.value_statement: React applications often mix local, shared, and server data without clear boundaries. This skill helps choose and apply Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, and React Query patterns.
actual_capabilities: Compares local, global, server, URL, and form state categories.
Provides Redux Toolkit store and slice patterns with typed hooks.
Shows scalable Zustand stores with slices and selective subscriptions.
Explains Jotai atoms, derived atoms, async atoms, and storage atoms.
Demonstrates React Query keys, cache timing, mutations, and optimistic updates.
Includes migration guidance from legacy Redux to Redux Toolkit.
limitations: It is a guidance document, not an automated code generator.
Examples use placeholder application APIs and types.
It does not benchmark performance for a specific application.
It does not audit authentication or token storage designs.

use_cases.title: Select a State Library
use_cases.description: Compare Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, and React Query for a new React application architecture.

use_cases.title: Refactor Shared State
use_cases.description: Move scattered component state into a typed global store with selectors and clearer ownership.

use_cases.title: Improve Server Data Flow
use_cases.description: Separate client UI state from cached API data using React Query patterns and optimistic updates.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a React state management approach for this app: [describe app size, data sources, and team experience].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Store
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate setup for shared client state
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a typed state store for [feature]. Include state shape, actions, selectors, and where each file should live.

prompt_templates.title: Separate Client and Server State
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React data flow and identify what should use React Query versus local or global client state: [paste summary].

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a migration plan from legacy Redux to Redux Toolkit for these slices and async flows: [list modules and risks].

output_examples.input: A small dashboard needs theme state, modal state, and cached user lists.
output_examples.output: Recommended approach: Zustand for UI state and React Query for user data. Keep theme and modal actions in a UI store. Let React Query own cache freshness, loading state, and refetch behavior.

output_examples.input: A large commerce app has cart, user, inventory, and checkout flows.
output: Use Redux Toolkit for cart and checkout workflows that need predictable actions.
Use React Query for inventory and user profile requests.
Avoid duplicating API responses in the global client store.

output_examples.input: A team wants granular updates for independent filters and preferences.
output_examples.output: Jotai is a strong fit when independent atoms reduce rerenders and simplify derived values. Persist only non-sensitive preferences.

best_practices: Keep state as close as possible to the components that use it.
Use selectors and typed hooks to reduce rerenders and runtime mistakes.
Separate server cache ownership from client UI state ownership.
anti_patterns: Do not put every value into a global store by default.
Do not duplicate server state across React Query and a client store.
Do not persist sensitive tokens in browser storage without a security review.

faq.question: Does this skill install any state libraries?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for libraries that developers install in their own projects.

faq.question: Can it help choose between Redux Toolkit and Zustand?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains selection criteria based on app size, complexity, team needs, and state ownership.

faq.question: Does it cover server state?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes React Query patterns for query keys, caching, mutations, and optimistic updates.

faq.question: Is it suitable for TypeScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples emphasize typed stores, typed hooks, and typed state shapes.

faq.question: Does it replace an application architecture review?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, but teams should still review project constraints and security requirements.

faq.question: Are the network examples risky?
faq.answer: The examples use relative API paths for teaching. Developers should review real endpoints and data handling in their applications.
